perturbation.original_text: The Company shall pay to the Consultant amounts shown on each such statement within thirty (30) days after receipt thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: The Company shall pay to the Consultant amounts shown on each such statement within sixty (60) days after receipt thereof.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the payment deadline to 60 days may violate Massachusetts prompt payment laws for consultants, which could be construed as employees for this purpose, although the contract states otherwise. Longer payment terms can be problematic under wage and hour laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: Massachusetts law requires prompt payment of wages. Although the contract specifies 'independent contractor' status, delayed payment significantly beyond typical payroll cycles can lead to a misclassification claim, making the 60-day period a violation of the spirit and potentially the letter of the wage payment law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XXI, Chapter 149, Section 148 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XXI LABOR AND INDUSTRIES Chapter 149 LABOR AND INDUSTRIES Section 148 Payment of wages; commissions; exemption by contract; persons deemed employers; provision for cashing check or draft; violation of statute Section 148 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 148: Payment of wages; commissions; exemption by contract; persons deemed employers; provision for cashing check or draft; violation of statute Section 148. Every person having employees in his service shall pay weekly or bi-weekly each such employee the wages earned by him to within six days of the termination of the pay period during which the wages were earned if employed for five or six days in a calendar week, or to within seven days of the termination of the pay period during which the wages were earned if such employee is employed seven days in a calendar week, or in the case of an employee who has worked for a period of less than five days, hereinafter called a casual employee, shall, within seven days after the termination of such period, pay the wages earned by such casual employee during such period, but any employee leaving his employment shall be paid in full on the following regular pay day, and, in the absence of a regular pay day, on the following Saturday; and any employee discharged from such employment shall be paid in full on the day of his discharge, or in Boston as soon as the laws requiring pay rolls, bills and accounts to be certified shall have been complied with; and the commonwealth, its departments, officers, boards and commissions shall so pay every mechanic, workman and laborer employed by it or them, and every person employed in any other capacity by it or them in any penal or charitable institution, and every county and city shall so pay every employee engaged in its business the wag
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the payment deadline to 60 days, which contradicts Massachusetts wage laws requiring prompt payment. The Perturbation and Law Explanations suggest this delay could lead to a misclassification claim violating wage payment laws. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that employees must be paid weekly or bi-weekly, within six or seven days of the pay period's end, depending on the number of days worked. While the contract specifies 'independent contractor' status, the delayed payment could lead to legal issues under wage and hour laws, making the 60-day payment term a violation of the law. The changed text creates a potential contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: During the Consultation Period (as defined below) and for a period of one year thereafter, except in connection with his performance of the Services, the Consultant shall not engage in any activity in the field described on Schedule A to this Agreement, and he shall not assist any other person or organization that engages in any such activity.
perturbation.changed_text: During the Consultation Period (as defined below) and for a period of two years thereafter, except in connection with his performance of the Services, the Consultant shall not engage in any activity in the field described on Schedule A to this Agreement, and he shall not assist any other person or organization that engages in any such activity.
perturbation.explanation: Increasing the non-compete period to two years might be deemed unreasonable and unenforceable under Massachusetts law, depending on the scope of the restriction and the consultant's role.
perturbation.law_explanation: Massachusetts law requires that non-compete agreements be reasonable in time and scope. Extending the non-compete period to two years, without additional consideration or a narrow definition of the prohibited activities, may render the clause unenforceable under M.G.L. c. 149, § 24L, particularly if it unduly restricts the consultant's ability to find work.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XXI, Chapter 149, Section 24L Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XXI LABOR AND INDUSTRIES Chapter 149 LABOR AND INDUSTRIES Section 24L Massachusetts Noncompetition Agreement Act Section 24L Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 24L: Massachusetts Noncompetition Agreement Act Section 24L. (a) As used in this section, the following words shall have the following meanings:- ''Business entity'', any person or group of persons performing or engaging in any activity, enterprise, profession or occupation for gain, benefit, advantage or livelihood, whether for profit or not for profit, including but not limited to corporations, limited liability companies, limited partnerships or limited liability partnerships. ''Employee'', an individual who is considered an employee under section 148B of this chapter; provided, however, that the term ''employee'', as used in this section, shall also include independent contractors under section 148B. ''Forfeiture agreement'', an agreement that imposes adverse financial consequences on a former employee as a result of the termination of an employment relationship, regardless of whether the employee engages in competitive activities following cessation of the employment relationship. Forfeiture agreements do not include forfeiture for competition agreements. ''Forfeiture for competition agreement'', an agreement that by its terms or through the manner in which it is enforced imposes adverse financial consequences on a former employee as a result of the termination of an employment relationship if the employee engages in competitive activities. ''Garden leave clause'', a provision within a noncompetition agreement by which an employer agrees to pay the employee during the restricted period, provided that such provision shall become effective upon termination of employment unless the restriction
perturbation.justification: The modified text extends the non-compete period from one year to two years. The Law Explanation states that under M.G.L. c. 149, § 24L, Massachusetts law requires non-compete agreements to be reasonable in time and scope, and that extending the period without additional consideration might render the clause unenforceable. The Perturbation Explanation also highlights that a two-year non-compete period might be unreasonable. Scraped Law Snippet 1, which references Section 24L, is the Massachusetts Noncompetition Agreement Act. Given that the modification increases the duration of the non-compete agreement which is a time-sensitive obligation, it may violate state law if deemed unreasonable, creating a legal contradiction under the 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' definition provided. Because the change might violate the act, it is more than a minor weakening; therefore, a contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: The Consultant may terminate the Consultation Period at any time upon thirty (30) days' written notice.
perturbation.changed_text: The Consultant may terminate the Consultation Period at any time upon ten (10) days' written notice.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the notice period for termination might violate Massachusetts' implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing if it prejudices the company by not allowing them enough time to find a replacement.
perturbation.law_explanation: While Chapter 93A primarily concerns consumer protection, the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing is applicable to all contracts in Massachusetts. Drastically shortening the notice period for termination could be construed as a violation of this covenant if it demonstrably harms the Company by depriving them of reasonable time to secure a replacement, potentially leading to legal challenges under Chapter 93A principles.

perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the notice period from 30 days to 10 days. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation state that drastically shortening the notice period could violate the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing under Massachusetts law, potentially harming the Company if it doesn't have enough time to find a replacement. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 focuses on consumer protection under Chapter 93A, the Law Explanation extends the principle of good faith to all contracts in Massachusetts. Thus, reducing the notice period creates a legal contradiction because it introduces a change that might be seen as violating the implied covenant of good faith.
